Chapter 26
Nucleosides, Nucleotides, and Nucleic Acids

Nucleic Acids
The mechanisms of heredity and genetics were discovered by Mendel in the 1850s
However, the molecular mechanism of heredity ( nucleic acids ) was discovered much later
Nucleic acids are polymers of nucleotides , which are composed of nucleosides , which contain a carbohydrate and a nitrogenous base
Two major types: deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) and ribonucleic acid (RNA)
The sequence of nucleotides in DNA represents the genetic code or genome of an organism

Section 26.1
PYRIMIDINES AND PURINES

Pyrimidines and Purines
Two general aromatic heterocycles are the basis of the nitrogenous bases in DNA and RNA

Pyrimidines and Purines-1
Hydroxy-substituted purines and pyrimidines exist mostly in their keto forms, despite their resemblance to phenols!
Consider resonance forms of the keto forms that explain this result.
Survey of Nitrogenous Bases
Survey of Nitrogenous Bases-1

Section 26.2
NUCLEOSIDES

Nucleosides
Recall that glycosylamines have N bound to the anomeric carbon of a monosaccharide
Nucleosides are glycosylamines with a nitrogenous base (or other aromatic heterocycle) as a substituent
In ribonucleic acids, ribose is the monosaccharide
In deoxyribonucleic acids, 2-deoxyribose is the monosaccharide
Survey of Nucleosides

Section 26.3
NUCLEOTIDES

Nucleotides
Phosphate ester linkages connect the nucleosides to one another in DNA
A nucleoside bearing one or more phosphate esters is called a nucleotide
Nucleotides are anionic at pH 7

ATP: A Biochemical Fuel
The chain of three phosphates in ATP is a highly energetic structure!
Hydrolysis to generate ADP and phosphate is very exergonic (more in the next section)
ATP is also used for phosphorylation , the conversion of –OH to a phosphate ester

Phosphorylation and Kinases
Phosphorylation is catalyzed by kinase enzymes
Phosphate is a decent leaving group—phosphorylated small molecules are activated toward nucleophiles

Cyclic Monophosphates
Cyclic phosphate esters that “bridge” the 5’ and 3’ positions of the sugar are important messaging molecules ( second messengers )

Section 26.4
BIOENERGETICS
Review of Thermodynamics

Section 26.5
ATP AND BIOENERGETICS

Hydrolysis of ATP
Hydrolysis of ATP is exergonic and provides energy for biochemical processes

ATP Hydrolysis as an Energy Source
Note that ATP must be involved mechanistically for the coupling process to work! Independent ATP hydrolysis is not enough!
Typically, phosphorylation of a nucleophilic group (–OH, –NHR, etc.) produces a good leaving group
For example, in the amidation of glutamic acid:
If ATP is an energy-rich compound, how do organisms synthesize it?

ATP Synthesis
The energy for ATP synthesis comes from metabolism of carbohydrates (glucose) and fats
The overall reaction is just the reverse of ATP hydrolysis, phosphorylation of ADP
Relative Energies of Biomolecules

Section 26.6
PHOSPHODIESTERS, OLIGONUCLEOTIDES, AND POLYNUCLEOTIDES

Phosphodiesters and Nucleic Acids
Nucleotide monomers are connected by phosphodiesters in nucleic acid polymers (analogous to the peptide bond in proteins)
The 3’ oxygen of one nucleotide is connected to the 5’ oxygen of the next via a phosphodiester
Nucleotides are added to the 3’ end of a nucleic acid chain in an elongation process
Can you see why the polymer is called a nucleic acid ?
Synthesis uses automated solid-phase techniques
